- The dominant spatial mode is more self-consistent in the neighborhood of 26 K than across the full T_p sweep, but the effect is quantitative rather than exact universality.

- Around 26 K, the dominant spatial mode is somewhat more coherent than it is globally, but the tested clocks do not convert that into a strong master-curve collapse. The data therefore support local structural similarity more than true temperature-independent universality.

- The structural evidence is stronger than a scalar Dip-depth collapse test because it uses the full DeltaM(T, t_w) map, but it is still limited by the sparse waiting-time grid and by missing low-T_p FM and high-T_p Dip clocks.

- Residual structure was quantified by the relative residual RMSE, the residual leading-mode explained variance, and coherence scores across temperature and waiting time. High residual coherence indicates a systematic missing correction rather than noise-like leftovers.
